question: When calculating the total width of an element in the CSS Box Model, what is included?
options: Only content is included
Only content + padding are included
Content + padding + border + margin are all included
Only content + margin are included
explanation: In the default CSS Box Model, the total space an element occupies is calculated as content width + padding + border + margin. When using box-sizing: border-box, the width includes content + padding + border, but margin is always calculated separately.

question: When box-sizing: border-box is set, what does the width property include?
options: Only content
Content + padding + border
Content + padding + border + margin
Only content + padding
explanation: When using box-sizing: border-box, the width and height include content + padding + border. This makes layout calculations more intuitive, and most CSS resets apply it globally. Margin is not included.

question: What is the default flex-direction value of a container with display: flex applied?
options: row
column
row-reverse
column-reverse
explanation: The default flex-direction of a Flexbox container is row. This means child elements are laid out horizontally (from left to right). Changing it to column will arrange them vertically.

question: In the following CSS, what is the actual rendered width of the .child element in pixels?
options: 200px
270px
250px
230px
explanation: With box-sizing: content-box (the default), the rendered width is content (200px) + left/right padding (20px x 2) + left/right border (5px x 2) = 250px. Margin is not included in the element's rendered width — it is external space.

question: What is the difference between align-items and justify-content in Flexbox?
options: Both handle main axis alignment but have different priority
align-items handles the main axis, and justify-content handles the cross axis
align-items applies to single elements, and justify-content applies only to multiple elements
justify-content handles the main axis, and align-items handles the cross axis
explanation: justify-content handles alignment along the main axis, while align-items handles alignment along the cross axis. When flex-direction is row, the main axis is horizontal and the cross axis is vertical. When flex-direction is column, it is the opposite.

question: What is the correct difference between position: absolute and position: fixed?
options: Both are viewport-relative, but absolute moves with scrolling
fixed is always relative to the body, and absolute is relative to the nearest relative parent
absolute is positioned relative to the nearest positioned ancestor, and fixed is positioned relative to the viewport
absolute is viewport-relative, and fixed is parent-relative
explanation: position: absolute is positioned relative to the nearest positioned ancestor (an ancestor whose position is not static). position: fixed is fixed relative to the viewport and stays in the same position even when scrolling. If there is no positioned ancestor, absolute also becomes viewport-relative.

question: What is the layout result for the .item elements in the following code?
options: All items are laid out in a single row with 150px width
Items stretch to 300px width with one item per row
Items stack vertically because of flex-wrap
Items are laid out 2 per row, and the rest wrap to the next line
explanation: flex: 0 0 150px means flex-grow: 0, flex-shrink: 0, flex-basis: 150px. Since the container width is 300px and each item is 150px, 2 items fit per row. With flex-wrap: wrap set, remaining items automatically wrap to the next line.

question: What is the correct behavior of grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(200px, 1fr)) in CSS Grid?
options: It creates as many columns as possible with a minimum of 200px to fit the container width, distributing remaining space equally
It always creates fixed 200px-wide columns
It creates only one column that takes the full width
It selects the larger value between 200px and 1fr for the column width
explanation: auto-fill creates as many tracks as can fit in the container. minmax(200px, 1fr) ensures each track is at least 200px and at most 1fr (equal distribution of remaining space). This pattern is a common approach for creating responsive grids without media queries.

question: What is the correct difference between auto-fill and auto-fit in CSS Grid?
options: auto-fill keeps empty tracks, while auto-fit collapses empty tracks and stretches items
auto-fit keeps empty tracks, while auto-fill collapses empty tracks
Both behave identically and only differ in name
auto-fill is used for fixed column counts, and auto-fit is used for responsive columns
explanation: auto-fill creates as many tracks as possible in the container, and empty tracks still occupy space. auto-fit collapses empty tracks to 0, distributing the remaining space to existing items. When there are enough items, both values behave identically.

question: You wrote the following CSS to center an element both vertically and horizontally, but vertical centering does not work. What is the cause?
options: justify-content and align-items cannot be used together
align-content: center should be used instead of align-items: center
display: grid must be used instead of display: flex
The flex container has no explicit height, so it is only as tall as its content
explanation: If no explicit height (or min-height) is set on the flex container, it only takes up as much height as its content. In this case, even with align-items: center applied, there is no vertical space to center within, so vertical centering appears to not work. You need to specify a height like height: 100vh.

question: What is the correct behavior of position: sticky?
options: It is always fixed to the viewport and does not move when scrolling
It only acts like absolute within its parent element
It toggles between relative and fixed depending on the scroll position
It does not work without JavaScript
explanation: position: sticky toggles between relative and fixed depending on the scroll position. Before the specified threshold (top, bottom, etc.) is reached, it behaves like relative; after reaching it, it behaves like fixed. However, it reverts to normal flow when it leaves the bounds of its parent element.

question: In the following CSS, what is the final width of .child?
options: 600px
0px
200px
300px
explanation: With flex-basis: 0 and flex-grow: 1, the child would try to take all available space (600px). However, since max-width: 200px is set, the final width is capped at 200px. max-width and min-width have higher priority than flex calculations.

question: What condition must an element meet for z-index to work?
options: Its position must be a value other than static, or it must be a flex/grid child
display: block must be set
The parent element must also have z-index set
The element must have explicit width and height
explanation: z-index only works on positioned elements (elements whose position is not static) or child elements of flex/grid containers. Setting z-index on a regular element with position: static will be ignored. It also works on elements that create a new stacking context.

question: In which situation does margin: 0 auto fail to horizontally center an element?
options: When the element has a background-color set
When the element is inline or inline-block
When the element has a border set
When the parent element has padding
explanation: margin: 0 auto works on block-level elements when an explicit width is set. Inline and inline-block elements only take up as much width as their content, so left and right auto margins compute to 0. In this case, you should use text-align: center on the parent or change the element to display: block.

question: What is the correct difference between display: none and visibility: hidden?
options: Both completely remove the element from the DOM
visibility: hidden removes the space, and display: none keeps the space
display: none removes the space, and visibility: hidden keeps the space
Both behave identically with only a performance difference
explanation: display: none completely removes the element from the render tree, so it takes up no space. visibility: hidden makes the element invisible but maintains its space in the layout. In both cases, the element still exists in the DOM.

question: In the following CSS, what is the actual margin gap between two adjacent .box elements?
options: 50px
20px
0px
30px
explanation: In CSS, vertical margins of adjacent block elements collapse (margin collapsing). Only the larger of the two margins is applied, so the actual gap between 30px and 20px is 30px. This only occurs with vertical margins and does not happen in flex/grid contexts.

question: What is the difference between explicit placement and implicit placement using grid-area in CSS Grid?
options: Explicit placement goes into areas defined by grid-template, while implicit placement goes into automatically generated tracks
Explicit placement requires JavaScript, while implicit placement works with CSS alone
Both are identical but differ in browser compatibility
Implicit placement works the same way as flex
explanation: The explicit grid consists of tracks defined by grid-template-rows/columns, where items are placed using grid-area and similar properties. Items that exceed the defined tracks are automatically placed in implicit tracks, whose sizes are controlled by grid-auto-rows/columns.

question: What is the most common cause of position: sticky not working?
options: The browser does not support sticky
The element does not have a width set
The element is not the first child
One of the ancestor elements has overflow: hidden set
explanation: The most common cause of position: sticky not working is when an ancestor element has overflow: hidden, auto, or scroll set. Sticky works within a scroll container, and if an ancestor has overflow set, that element becomes the scroll container, causing unexpected behavior.

question: What does flex: 1 mean in Flexbox?
options: flex-grow: 1, flex-shrink: 1, flex-basis: 0
flex-grow: 0, flex-shrink: 1, flex-basis: auto
flex-grow: 1, flex-shrink: 0, flex-basis: auto
flex-grow: 1, flex-shrink: 1, flex-basis: 100%
explanation: flex: 1 is a shorthand for flex-grow: 1, flex-shrink: 1, flex-basis: 0. This means the element will equally share available space and can also shrink when needed. Unlike flex: auto (flex-basis: auto), it distributes space regardless of content size.

question: What is the priority when min-width, max-width, and width conflict?
options: width > max-width > min-width
max-width > min-width > width
min-width > max-width > width
If min-width is greater than max-width, min-width wins; otherwise, max-width takes priority over width
explanation: According to the CSS specification: 1) If min-width is greater than max-width, min-width wins. 2) Otherwise, max-width takes priority over width (if width is greater than max-width, max-width is applied). 3) min-width takes priority over width (if width is less than min-width, min-width is applied).

question: What is the correct role of the CSS gap property?
options: It sets the spacing between items in a flex/grid container
It sets the outer margin of an element
It sets the inner padding of an element
It prevents margin collapsing between adjacent block elements
explanation: The gap property sets the spacing between child items in a flex or grid container. Unlike margin, it does not create spacing on the outside of the first/last items, and margin collapsing issues do not occur, making it much more intuitive for managing layout spacing.

question: In the following CSS, what is the height of .box in pixels?
options: 400px
200px
800px
100px
explanation: aspect-ratio: 2 / 1 means the width-to-height ratio is 2:1. Since the width is 400px, the height is 400px / 2 = 200px. aspect-ratio only applies when no explicit height is set, and it is very useful for responsive media containers.

question: What is the correct difference between place-items and place-content in CSS Grid?
options: Both have the same functionality and only differ in name
place-items aligns individual items within their cells, while place-content aligns the entire set of tracks within the container
place-content aligns individual items, while place-items aligns the entire grid
place-items handles only horizontal alignment, while place-content handles only vertical alignment
explanation: place-items is a shorthand for align-items + justify-items, which aligns each item within its own cell. place-content is a shorthand for align-content + justify-content, which aligns the entire group of tracks within the container. The difference becomes clear when the container has extra space.

question: How do you fix the problem where a parent element's height collapses to 0 when it contains floated children?
options: Apply position: absolute to the parent
Remove float from children and add margin instead
Apply overflow: hidden or display: flow-root to the parent
Apply z-index: 1 to the parent
explanation: Floated elements are taken out of the normal flow, so the parent does not recognize the height of its children. To fix this, you need to create a new Block Formatting Context (BFC) on the parent. overflow: hidden or display: flow-root creates a BFC that contains the floated children.

question: In the following CSS using grid-template-areas, what area does .sidebar occupy?
options: Second row of the first column, 200px wide
Second row of the second column, 1fr wide
Spans all rows in the first column
Occupies the entire second row
explanation: In grid-template-areas, 'sidebar' is placed in the first column of the second row. Since the first column is defined as 200px in grid-template-columns, the sidebar's width is 200px. Using grid-area makes it easy to visually understand the layout structure.

question: In the following CSS with 3 .item elements, what is the final width of each item?
options: Base size 200px with 0px remaining to distribute, so all stay at 200px
explanation: The total flex-basis is 200px x 3 = 600px, which exactly matches the container width. Since there is no remaining space, the flex-grow ratio is irrelevant, and each item keeps its flex-basis of 200px. flex-grow only matters when there is extra space to distribute.

question: What is the correct difference between overflow: auto and overflow: scroll?
options: Both always show scrollbars
auto only shows scrollbars when content overflows, while scroll always shows scrollbars
scroll only shows scrollbars when content overflows, while auto always shows them
auto only allows horizontal scrolling, while scroll only allows vertical scrolling
explanation: overflow: auto shows scrollbars only when content exceeds the element's size. overflow: scroll always reserves space for and displays scrollbars, even when content does not overflow. In most cases, auto provides a better user experience.

question: What causes the layout to break in the following CSS using calc()?
options: The subtraction operator cannot be used inside calc()
Missing spaces around the operator inside calc() causes a parsing error
Mixing % and px units is not allowed
calc() is not supported on the width property
explanation: In the calc() function, the addition (+) and subtraction (-) operators must have spaces on both sides. calc(100%-250px) causes a parsing error and must be written as calc(100% - 250px). Spaces are not strictly required for multiplication (*) and division (/), but they are recommended for readability.

question: Why do unintended gaps appear between inline-block elements?
options: The browser adds default margin
Padding is automatically applied to inline-block
Whitespace (line breaks/spaces) in the HTML source code is rendered
It is a bug in display: inline-block
explanation: Inline-block elements are treated like inline elements, so whitespace (line breaks, tabs, spaces) between tags in the HTML source is rendered as approximately 4px of space. To fix this, you can apply font-size: 0 to the parent, use flexbox/grid, or write the HTML tags without whitespace between them.

question: What is the correct difference between CSS contain: layout and contain: size?
options: contain: layout isolates the element's internal layout from affecting the outside, while contain: size makes the element's size independent of its children
contain: size isolates the layout, while contain: layout fixes the size
Both serve to completely hide the element
contain: layout is only for GPU acceleration, while contain: size is only for CPU optimization
explanation: contain: layout isolates internal layout changes from affecting external elements. contain: size makes the element's size determination ignore child elements, requiring explicit sizing. Both properties help the browser reduce the scope of layout recalculations, improving rendering performance.

question: What is the correct role of the order property in Flexbox?
options: It changes the DOM order of elements, also affecting accessibility
It changes only the visual layout order without modifying the DOM structure
Elements with a higher order value are placed first
order only works in grid and is ignored in flex
explanation: The order property changes the visual layout order of flex/grid items but keeps the DOM order unchanged. The default value is 0, and items with smaller values are placed first. Note that screen readers follow the DOM order, so accessibility is not affected by the visual reordering.

question: In the following CSS, what is the final width of each item when the container is 300px wide?
options: 200px, 200px (overflow)
explanation: The total flex-basis (400px) exceeds the container (300px), so 100px must be shrunk. Both items have flex-shrink: 1 and the same flex-basis, so the 100px deficit is distributed equally — each shrinks by 50px to become 150px. flex-shrink calculates shrinkage proportionally based on flex-basis.

question: What is the correct difference between object-fit: cover and object-fit: contain?
options: cover maintains aspect ratio while filling the container (may crop), while contain maintains aspect ratio and scales down to show the entire image
contain crops the image to fill the container, while cover shows the entire image
cover stretches the image ignoring aspect ratio, while contain maintains aspect ratio
Both behave identically with only a performance difference
explanation: object-fit: cover maintains the image's aspect ratio while completely filling the container. If the ratios don't match, parts of the image will be cropped. object-fit: contain maintains the aspect ratio while ensuring the entire image is visible within the container, which may result in letterboxing (empty space).

question: A child element has z-index: 9999 but appears below another section's element with z-index: 1. What is the cause?
options: z-index does not support values up to 9999
The parent element creates a new stacking context, so the child's z-index only applies within the parent's scope
The position property is not set
z-index only supports negative values
explanation: When a parent element has opacity < 1, transform, filter, isolation: isolate, etc., it creates a new stacking context. In this case, the child's z-index is only effective within that stacking context, and when compared with elements in other stacking contexts, the parent's z-index is what matters.

question: What is the correct role of the align-self property in Flexbox?
options: It sets the cross-axis alignment for all children of the container
It individually sets the main axis alignment of an element
It overrides the container's align-items value for an individual flex item's cross-axis alignment
It sets the external alignment of the flex container itself
explanation: align-self is applied to individual flex items to override the container's align-items value for that specific item. For example, even if the container has align-items: flex-start, setting align-self: flex-end on a specific item will place only that item at the end of the cross axis.

question: In the following CSS Grid, what area does .item occupy?
options: Column 2, row 1 — 100px × 80px
Columns 2–3, row 1 — 200px × 80px
Column 2, rows 1–2 — 100px × 160px
Columns 2–3, rows 1–2 — 200px × 160px
explanation: grid-column: 2 / 4 spans from the 2nd grid line to the 4th line, covering columns 2 and 3 (100px + 100px = 200px). grid-row: 1 / 3 spans from the 1st line to the 3rd line, covering rows 1 and 2 (80px + 80px = 160px). The item occupies a 200px × 160px area.

question: What type of elements is the CSS object-fit property used on?
options: It can be used on any block element
It specifies how replaced elements like img and video fit within their container
It adjusts the size of flex container children
It specifies overflow handling for text elements
explanation: object-fit controls how the content of replaced elements like img, video, and canvas fits within the specified width and height. Available values include fill, contain, cover, none, and scale-down. It is similar in concept to background-size but applies to the element's content itself.

question: In the following flex container, which item is visually placed furthest to the left?
options: .a (order: 2)
.c (order: 0)
.b (order: -1)
.a is first following DOM order
explanation: Flex items are placed in ascending order of their order value. .b (order: -1) has the smallest value and is placed furthest to the left, followed by .c (order: 0), then .a (order: 2) on the far right. The default order value is 0, and negative values are allowed.

question: grid-column: span 2 is applied to a grid item but it does not span 2 columns. What is the most likely cause?
options: The span keyword can only be used with grid-row
grid-column is only supported in IE
display: grid is not set on the container
The container's grid-template-columns only defines 1 column
explanation: grid-column: span 2 makes an item span 2 columns, but if the container's grid-template-columns only defines 1 column, there aren't enough tracks to span across. Implicit tracks may be generated, but without sufficient columns defined in grid-template-columns, the layout may not behave as expected.

question: What is the correct difference between CSS Container Queries and Media Queries?
options: Container Queries apply styles based on the parent container's size, while Media Queries apply styles based on the viewport size
Media Queries are container-based, and Container Queries are viewport-based
Container Queries require JavaScript, while Media Queries work with CSS alone
Both are viewport-based, but Container Queries have better performance
explanation: Media Queries conditionally apply styles based on the viewport (browser window) size. Container Queries (@container) apply styles based on the size of the nearest containment context (an ancestor with container-type set). This enables truly component-based responsive design where components respond based on where they are placed.
